figures.caption: FIG. 4. Plots of the kinetic term magnitude |Vx|2+ |Vy|2. Left: Standard Wilson-overlap. Center: Brillouinoverlap with characteristic flat doubler basins. Right: The learned operator.
FIG. 5. Plots of the mass term M. Left: Standard Wilson-overlap. Center: Brillouin-overlap with characteristic flat doubler basins. Right: The learned operator.
FIG. 2. The effective sign function ϵeff (HW ) extracted from the learned operator, plotted against the eigenvalues of the original Wilson Hamiltonian HW . The network realizes a sharp step function.

FIG. 3. Magnitude of the real-space hopping parameters |D(r)| of the learned 2D operator as a function of the distance r. The observed exponential-like suppression provides a numerical check of the locality properties of the learned operator.

FIG. 8. The learned eigenvalue spectrum in 2D. Guided by the generalized polynomial constraint, spatial locality, and doubler-decoupling conditions, the network selects sglobal → 1.001 (c1 = 1.001) and zeroes out higher-order coefficients (cn>1 = 0.000), indicating that the standard GW relation is selected as the preferred solution within the present polynomial optimization setup.

FIG. 6. The 4D spectrum of the learned operator. Left: The original 4D Wilson Dirac spectrum (input). Right: The spectrum of the learned 4D operator (output), which lies close to the Ginsparg-Wilson circle. While m0 is randomized during training, the spectrum is evaluated here at m0 = −1.0.
FIG. 7. The effective sign function ϵ(HW ) extracted from the 4D learned operator, where the network realizes a step function.

FIG. 1. Comparison of the eigenvalue spectrum in the complex plane. Left: The original 2D Wilson Dirac spectrum (input). Right: The spectrum of the learned 2D operator (output), which collapses onto the Ginsparg-Wilson circle. While m0 is randomized during training, the spectrum is evaluated here at m0 = −1.0.

FIG. 9. The learned eigenvalue spectrum corresponding to the autonomous discovery of the generalized Fujikawa relation. By initializing the search with a slight bias toward the n = 2 term, the network zeroes out all other coefficients and precisely converges to c2 = 0.250, cn̸=2 = 0, showing that the same framework can converge to an alternative higher-order GW-type algebraic structure.
